summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

If T is a linear transformation, what is T(0)?

For any linear transformation T, T(0) = T(0 * v) = 0 * T(v) = 0. This is a direct consequence of the homogeneity property and serves as a quick check for linearity.

If T: R^3 -> R^2 is defined by T(x, y, z) = (x + y, y - z), what is T(1, 2, 3)?

Substituting x=1, y=2, z=3 gives T(1,2,3) = (1+2, 2-3) = (3, -1). This is a straightforward evaluation of the linear transformation at a specific input vector.

If T: R^2 -> R^2 is linear and T(1,0) = (2,3) and T(0,1) = (1,-1), what is T(2,5)?

By linearity, T(2,5) = 2T(1,0) + 5T(0,1) = 2(2,3) + 5(1,-1) = (4,6) + (5,-5) = (9,1). A linear transformation is completely determined by its action on a basis.

If T is a linear transformation and T(v) = w, what is T(3v)?

By the homogeneity property of linear transformations, T(3v) = 3T(v) = 3w. Scalar multiplication on the input is preserved and becomes scalar multiplication on the output.

If T: R^2 -> R is defined by T(x,y) = 3x - 2y, what is T(4, 5)?

T(4,5) = 3(4) - 2(5) = 12 - 10 = 2. This transformation maps R^2 to R by taking a linear combination of the two input components.

What is a linear transformation?

A linear transformation is a function T: V -> W between vector spaces that preserves the two fundamental operations: vector addition and scalar multiplication. This algebraic structure-preserving property is the defining characteristic.

What is the 'domain' of a linear transformation T: V -> W?

The domain of T: V -> W is the vector space V, which is the set of all possible input vectors. Every vector in V can be mapped through T to produce an output in W.

What is the 'codomain' of a linear transformation T: V -> W?

The codomain of T: V -> W is the vector space W. It is the space in which the outputs live, though not every element of W necessarily appears as an output (that subset is the image or range).

What is an 'isomorphism' in the context of linear transformations?

An isomorphism is a linear transformation that is both injective (one-to-one) and surjective (onto). It establishes a perfect correspondence between two vector spaces, showing they are structurally identical.

What is the 'zero transformation'?

The zero transformation T(v) = 0 for all v maps every vector in the domain to the zero vector in the codomain. It is always linear since T(u+v) = 0 = 0+0 = T(u)+T(v) and T(cv) = 0 = c*0 = cT(v).

A physics experiment measures force output T(x) for input displacement x. The lab finds T(2) = 10 and T(5) = 25. If the system is linear, what is T(7)?

Since T is linear and T(2)=10, we get T(1)=5, so T(x)=5x. Therefore T(7)=5(7)=35. Alternatively, 7=2+5 so T(7)=T(2)+T(5)=10+25=35 by additivity.

A factory control system takes input voltage v and produces output current T(v). Tests show T(3) = 12 and T(7) = 28. An engineer claims the system is linear. If true, what is T(10)?

From T(3)=12, we get T(1)=4, so T(v)=4v. Checking: T(7)=4(7)=28, which is consistent. Therefore T(10)=4(10)=40. Alternatively, T(10)=T(3)+T(7)=12+28=40.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A factory control system takes input voltage v and produces output current T(v). Tests show T(3) = 12 and T(7) = 28. An engineer claims the system is linear. If true, what is T(10)?

From T(3)=12, we get T(1)=4, so T(v)=4v. Checking: T(7)=4(7)=28, which is consistent. Therefore T(10)=4(10)=40. Alternatively, T(10)=T(3)+T(7)=12+28=40.
